div.head: VISIT REPORT
ICL Slough
CAFS working party
21 March 1985
div.p: A consolidated list of the current 76 outstanding gripes about CAFS and its interfaces not already categorised as A (i.e. on the committed development path) or B (i.e. impossible with the current hardware) was tabled, but only discussed long enough for me to add one more gripe. H. Carmichael is to produce a new consolidated list with some notes on each for ranking independently by the WP and by ICL; this exercise to be completed by next meeting (early May).
The appalling D. Lovegrove's proposals for a new "CAFS overview" manual were discussed. I have a copy of the draft contents of what should be quite a useful little publication (if it ever happens).

J. Finch reported that the massive document on VME performance currently in its final stages of revision includes as much information about monitoring CAFS performance as anyone currently was letting on; working party members were offered copies of said tome (I said yes please). On the two aspects which specifically interested us, namely (a) how can you tell how busy the CAFS unit actually is and (b) how can you charge for its use differentially, there seems to be little progress; it is strongly suspected that the answer to both questions is "dunno".
Volunteers were requested (a) to join the CUA executive (b) to give presentations at the next two CUA CAFS Lunches (Nov 85 and May 86).

The bulk of the day was taken up with discussion of the two day CAFS Exploitation seminar to be dry-run next month, under the aegis of Dick Jukes. The course is aimed at analysts rather than programmers and covers more or less the expected ground. Much of it is necessarily more to do with Querymaster and RCI as software tools than with CAFS as such, but it does include a session on CSO and (at the end) a gray area labelled DCI. I argued, probably too forcibly, that CSO was the best place to start, following on more naturally than QM from an initial session about what CAFS actually is and how it works, thus endearing myself to the inventor of CSO who was (unbeknownst to me) present, if to no one else.
Working Party business consisted mainly of shortlisting the sites to be featured in the Case Studies section of the report and reviewing progress on the other sections (not a lot).
